g.data-narration: Welcome to Knowledge Representation Issues in AI. This topic covers how AI systems represent and reason with knowledge.
Knowledge Representation is how AI systems symbolically encode knowledge about the world in a form that enables computational reasoning and problem solving.
g.text: KR is how AI systems symbolically encode knowledge about the world
in a form that enables computational reasoning and problem solving.
Core purpose: Knowledge Acquisition → Inference → Utilization

g.data-narration: KR serves as surrogates for real-world objects, makes ontological commitments about existence of concepts, and provides medium for efficient computation.
g.text: • Surrogates: Representation substitutes for the real world
• Ontological Commitments: Defines concepts and relationships
• Medium for Efficient Computation & Human Expression

g.data-narration: Representational adequacy asks if the representation can capture all necessary knowledge. Inferential adequacy checks if we can derive new sound conclusions.
g.text: Can the representation capture ALL necessary
Example: "The book is on the table" is easy.
Can we derive NEW logically sound conclusions

g.data-narration: Inferential efficiency measures how fast we can derive new knowledge. Acquisitional efficiency measures how easy it is to add new knowledge to the system.
g.text: How EASY and FAST is it to derive new
Propositional logic is adequate but slow.
How EASY is it to ADD new knowledge?
Can domain experts (not engineers) input knowledge?

g.data-narration: Declarative knowledge states what is true. Procedural knowledge describes how to do something. Modern AI systems use hybrid approaches combining both types.
g.text: Declarative: "WHAT is true" - Facts, relationships, logic statements
Procedural: "HOW to do something" - Algorithms, rules, procedures
Modern systems use HYBRID approaches combining both!

g.data-narration: Declarative knowledge is readable, modular, and easy to modify but inference can be slow. Procedural knowledge is efficient and encodes heuristics but is hard to understand and modify.
g.text: Example: "The capital of France is Paris"

g.data-narration: There is always a trade-off between expressiveness and tractability. More expressive formalisms like higher-order logic have lower computational efficiency.
Logical representations use propositional and predicate logic with clear semantics and sound inference but are computationally inefficient and struggle with uncertainty.
g.text: ✓ Clear semantics, high representational adequacy, sound inference

g.data-narration: Production rules use IF-THEN statements that are highly modular and easy for domain experts to create but face rule interaction conflicts.
g.text: ✓ Highly modular, acquisitionally efficient, natural for heuristics
✗ Rule interaction conflicts, poor for structured knowledge, control flow issues

g.data-narration: Frames represent structured objects with slots and inheritance but face multiple inheritance conflicts and poor handling of unexpected cases.
g.text: ✓ Excellent for prototypical knowledge, default expectations, inheritance
✗ Multiple inheritance conflicts, semantics messy, poor with unexpected cases
Example: Frame "Dog" with slots: legs=4, sound="bark", color varies

g.data-narration: Semantic networks use graphs with nodes and links for intuitive representation but lack standardized formal semantics and can become computationally complex.
g.text: ✓ Intuitive graphical representation, efficient for relational queries
✗ No standardized formal semantics, can become computationally complex

g.data-narration: Knowledge representation must handle incomplete knowledge, uncertain information, and vague concepts using probabilistic reasoning, fuzzy logic, or non-monotonic logic.
g.text: • Incomplete Knowledge: We lack information (Is it raining in Timbuktu?)
• Uncertain Knowledge: Info not fully reliable (70% chance of rain)
Solutions: Probabilistic Reasoning, Fuzzy Logic, Non-Monotonic Logic

g.data-narration: A good KR system needs clear syntax and semantics, soundness, ontological minimality, naturalness for humans, and balance between expressiveness and tractability.
g.text: Properties of a Good KR System

g.data-narration: In summary, KR balances representational and inferential adequacy with efficiency considerations. Modern systems use hybrid approaches and handle uncertainty with specialized logics.
g.text: 1. KR balances Representational vs Inferential Adequacy with Efficiency
2. Declarative (what) vs Procedural (how) - modern systems use HYBRID
3. No perfect formalism exists - trade-offs are inevitable
4. Handle uncertainty with probabilistic/fuzzy logic extensions

g.data-narration: For exams, remember that ease of use relates to acquisitional efficiency while deriving new facts relates to inferential adequacy. Always use concrete examples and compare formalisms.
g.text: Ease of use relates to Acquisitional Efficiency
Deriving new facts relates to Inferential Adequacy
